summary: DHH argues that programmer productivity should be optimized over computational efficiency, since programmers are far more expensive than computing resources. He calculates that one programmer's salary equals roughly 3,663 server cores, making even small productivity gains enormously valuable in silicon terms. This has been Ruby on Rails' philosophy for twenty years: trade cheap compute time for expensive programmer time. He acknowledges the aesthetic appeal of squeezing maximum performance from hardware but argues it rarely makes economic sense for business software. The post positions this argument in the context of AI hype, suggesting that productivity tools like Rails have been solving the same economic problem AI promises to address.
key_points: One $200K/year programmer costs the equivalent of 3,663 AMD EPYC cores, making bio core optimization dramatically more cost-effective than silicon optimization
Many programmers have a misplaced moral sympathy for silicon efficiency that overrides the economic math favoring their own productivity
Ruby on Rails has been betting on programmer productivity over machine efficiency for 20 years, and the economics have only gotten more favorable
AI excitement in companies is driven by the same economic logic Rails always exploited: reducing the cost of expensive human programmers
Most business software doesn't need heroic optimization — computers have been fast enough for that work for years
money_quotes: Programmers continuing to become more expensive, computers continuing to become less so. Therefore, the smart bet was on making those programmers more productive EVEN AT THE EXPENSE OF THE COMPUTER!
They are in effect very expensive biological computing cores, and the real scarce resource.
The price of one biological core is the same as the price of 3663 silicon cores.
If you manage to make the bio core 10% more efficient, you will have saved the equivalent cost of 366 silicon cores.
Many of these squishy, biological programming cores have a distinctly human sympathy for their silicon counterparts that overrides the math.
There is something beautifully wholesome about making computers do more with fewer resources.
Most software around the world is priced on their inputs, not on the silicon it requires.
Best you deal in the economics of the present day, pick the most productive tool chain available to you, and bet that happy programmers will be the best bang for your buck.
key_insight: Since one programmer costs as much as thousands of server cores, even marginal improvements to programmer productivity vastly outweigh any gains from optimizing computational efficiency.
